title: TCP Retransmission
description: TCP's mechanism for recovering lost segments by retransmitting unacknowledged data.
fullExplanation: TCP guarantees delivery by retransmitting any segment that isn't acknowledged within a timeout period. The RTO (Retransmission Timeout) is calculated dynamically based on measured RTT (Round Trip Time) using Karn's algorithm. TCP also uses fast retransmit — if 3 duplicate ACKs are received, the lost segment is retransmitted immediately without waiting for the RTO. SACK (Selective Acknowledgement) allows the receiver to acknowledge non-contiguous blocks, enabling the sender to retransmit only the missing segments.

mechanisms.description: Timer started when segment sent. If ACK not received before RTO, segment retransmitted. RTO doubles on each timeout (exponential backoff).
3 duplicate ACKs indicate a gap in received data. Retransmit immediately without waiting for RTO.
Receiver reports which blocks were received. Sender retransmits only missing segments, not everything after the gap.
Receiver reports duplicate segments received — helps sender detect spurious retransmissions.

rtoCalculation.description: RTO calculated from smoothed RTT (SRTT) and RTT variance (RTTVAR)
rtoCalculation.backoff: RTO doubles on each consecutive timeout (exponential backoff, max ~64s)

keyFacts: RTO minimum: 1 second (RFC 6298) — prevents spurious retransmissions
Karn's algorithm: don't update RTT estimate using retransmitted segments
SACK option: negotiated in SYN/SYN-ACK — most modern stacks support it
Spurious retransmission: segment retransmitted unnecessarily (RTO too aggressive)
TCP Timestamp option: enables more accurate RTT measurement
Retransmission storm: many simultaneous timeouts → network collapse (TCP meltdown)
Wireshark: 'TCP Retransmission' and 'TCP Fast Retransmission' annotations
